title: Aged Microbiota Increases Post-Fracture Pain and Delays Fracture Healing

summary.hook: This mouse study tests whether an aged gut microbiota, independent of the mouse’s chronological age, worsens recovery after femoral fracture.
summary.summary_30s: This mouse study tests whether an aged gut microbiota, independent of the mouse’s chronological age, worsens recovery after femoral fracture.

audience_versions.general: This mouse study tests whether an aged gut microbiota, independent of the mouse’s chronological age, worsens recovery after femoral fracture.
audience_versions.undergrad: This mouse study tests whether an aged gut microbiota, independent of the mouse’s chronological age, worsens recovery after femoral fracture. Experimental animal study with microbiome manipulation (antibiotics \+ FMT) and fracture model.. n=9–13 per group (randomized) If you are new to research, this poster is a good example of how a question becomes a study and then a practical takeaway.

audience_versions.community: This mouse study tests whether an aged gut microbiota, independent of the mouse’s chronological age, worsens recovery after femoral fracture. Microbiota composition (age-associated) may be a modifiable contributor to post-fracture pain and healing outcomes, particularly in females. For community partners, the key question is who benefits, who might be left out, and how the work could translate into real-world impact.
audience_versions.leader: This mouse study tests whether an aged gut microbiota, independent of the mouse’s chronological age, worsens recovery after femoral fracture. Microbiota composition (age-associated) may be a modifiable contributor to post-fracture pain and healing outcomes, particularly in females. For leaders, the main lens is whether this work is ready for scale, support, implementation, or further investment within brain, behavior & aging.

talktrack_2m.why_it_matters: Microbiota composition (age-associated) may be a modifiable contributor to post-fracture pain and healing outcomes, particularly in females.

key_numbers: Antibiotics 14 days; FMT described as 7 days (via gavage) and also shown as “FMT for 14 days” in the schematic; fracture...
Young vs aged microbiota did not significantly affect body weight recovery post-fracture; both sexes returned to...
In females, Aged-FMT decreased callus bone content (lower BV, BV/TV, BMD vs Young-FMT; *P<0.05).
Aged microbiome increased CD3+ T-cell populations in DRG at day 7 post-fracture; CD11b+ populations did not differ.
takeaways: Fracture is associated with dramatic alterations in gut microbiota composition; stacked-bar plots show top-10 genera changes from Day 0 to Days 3, 10, 14, 18 in young and...
Young vs aged microbiota does not significantly affect body weight recovery following fracture; both sexes show an initial weight drop after fracture and recovery to...
Pain behaviors: In females, young microbiome pain/nociception measures return to pre-fracture levels faster than aged microbiota; young-FMT accelerates recovery vs...

general: What is the main takeaway you hope attendees remember from this poster?
What surprised you most once you started digging into this topic?
Why does this work matter beyond this specific poster session?
undergrad: What part of this project would be most helpful for an undergraduate student to learn from?
What skills or tools were most important for getting this project done?
If an undergraduate wanted to get involved in work like this, where would you suggest they start?
grad: In Fig. 2, can you walk through the exact timeline—antibiotics duration, FMT duration, and when fracture occurred relative to FMT (and why the poster shows both 7 and 14 days)?
What was the key design or analysis tradeoff in this project?
If you were extending this into a next study, what would you tighten or measure differently?
researcher: In Fig. 2, can you walk through the exact timeline—antibiotics duration, FMT duration, and when fracture occurred relative to FMT (and why the poster shows both 7 and 14 days)?
What were the donor sources for Young-FMT (12-wk) and Aged-FMT (18-mo)—how many donors and how were samples pooled?
How did you verify microbiome depletion and engraftment after antibiotics and FMT (sequencing timepoints, metrics)?
clinician: How could these findings affect clinical care, screening, or decision-making in practice?
What kind of patient or care setting would this poster be most relevant for?
What would need to happen before this work would be ready for broader clinical use?
community: Who in the community is most likely to benefit if this work moves forward?
What kinds of community partnerships would strengthen the next phase of this project?
What would meaningful real-world impact look like for the people touched by this work?
leader: What type of support, infrastructure, or investment would help this work move further?
What is the clearest implementation or scale-up opportunity that comes out of this poster?
If a leader wanted to back the next step, what outcome would be most important to track?
